sections.plain_summary: Parasitic infection occurs when parasites—organisms that live in or on your body and feed from it—enter your system, usually through contaminated food, water, soil, or insect bites. Symptoms vary widely depending on the parasite type and can range from digestive upset to fatigue, rashes, or anemia. Most parasitic infections are treatable with medication, though some may persist if untreated.
sections.detailed_summary: Parasitic infections are caused by single-celled protozoa, flatworms, roundworms, or other parasites that colonize the intestines, blood, tissues, or organs. Transmission routes differ by parasite: some spread through contaminated food or water (giardia, cryptosporidium), others via soil contact (hookworms, roundworms), insect vectors (malaria, sleeping sickness), or sexual contact (trichomoniasis). Once established, parasites can cause chronic inflammation, nutrient malabsorption, immune dysregulation, and systemic symptoms. Prevalence is highest in tropical and subtropical regions with poor sanitation, but parasitic infections also occur in developed countries. Diagnosis typically requires stool samples, blood tests, or serological testing. Treatment is usually effective with antiparasitic medications, though some infections require prolonged therapy or multiple rounds.

prevalence.summary: Billions of people worldwide carry parasitic infections; prevalence is highest in low- and middle-income countries with poor water and sanitation.
prevalence.estimate: 1.5–2 billion people globally infected; 0.1–1% of people in developed countries
prevalence.notes: Actual prevalence in developed nations is likely underestimated due to low clinical suspicion and underdiagnosis. Rates vary dramatically by geography, access to clean water, sanitation, and public health infrastructure. Tropical and subtropical regions report much higher infection rates.

subgroups.name: Intestinal parasites
subgroups.description: Parasites that colonize the small or large intestine, causing malabsorption and digestive symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Stool often contains visible parasites or eggs
Primarily causes digestive and nutritional symptoms
Transmitted via fecal-oral route, contaminated food or water
Examples: Giardia, Entamoeba, hookworms, roundworms, tapeworms

subgroups.name: Tissue and blood parasites
subgroups.description: Parasites that invade deeper tissues, blood, or organs rather than remaining in the intestine.
distinguishing_features: Often requires blood tests or imaging for diagnosis
May involve vector transmission (mosquitoes, ticks, snails)
Examples: malaria, schistosomiasis, filariasis, sleeping sickness

subgroups.name: Protozoan infections
subgroups.description: Single-celled parasitic infections, often harder to detect and more persistent.
distinguishing_features: May be resistant to standard antiparasitic drugs

subgroups.name: Helminth (worm) infections
subgroups.description: Multi-cellular parasitic worms that can grow large and cause mechanical damage.
distinguishing_features: Parasites often visible in stool samples
Can cause iron-deficiency anemia and protein malnutrition

symptoms.notes: Often watery or greasy; common with intestinal parasites like giardia. May be chronic and intermittent.
Can range from mild discomfort to severe cramping, worse after eating. May be colicky.
Less common but occurs with some worm infections, particularly tapeworms.
Due to malabsorption and fermentation of undigested food by parasites.
May lead to unintentional weight loss over time.
Often due to nutrient malabsorption, anemia, or chronic inflammation. May be severe and debilitating.
Iron-deficiency anemia common with blood-feeding parasites like hookworms. May cause fatigue and dizziness.
Particularly common with pinworms (Enterobius); often worse at night.
May indicate tissue parasites or allergic reaction; can be itchy and migratory.
Due to immune response or parasites in muscle tissue; may mimic arthritis.
More common with blood parasites like malaria or dengue; may be intermittent or cyclical.
Indicates immune activation; can occur with many parasitic infections.
Some parasites (e.g., roundworms) migrate through the lungs; typically self-limited.
Due to malnutrition, chronic inflammation, or in some cases direct parasitic involvement.
Parasites damage intestinal lining, increasing food sensitivities. Often resolve after treatment.

symptoms.symptom: Visible parasites or segments in stool
symptoms.notes: More common with larger worms; may appear like rice grains or cucumber seeds.

key_patterns: Chronic diarrhea or loose stools combined with weight loss and fatigue despite adequate food intake
Symptoms that wax and wane, with periods of relative wellness followed by flare-ups
Digestive symptoms accompanied by iron-deficiency anemia (pallor, shortness of breath, fatigue)
Itching around the anus or genitals, especially at night, sometimes visible tiny worms in stool or underwear
Skin rashes, hives, or welts that appear and migrate without clear allergen trigger
Symptoms that started after travel to tropical regions or consumption of untreated water or raw food
Unexplained eosinophilia (high eosinophil count) on bloodwork combined with digestive or skin symptoms

quick_checks.check: Inspect stool for visible parasites or segments (rice-like or cucumber-seed-like pieces)
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Visible parasites strongly suggest helminth infection and warrant immediate medical evaluation. Absence does not rule out infection.

quick_checks.check: Note timing of symptoms relative to travel, eating habits, or water exposure
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Symptoms appearing 1–3 weeks after travel to endemic areas or after consuming untreated water suggest parasitic transmission.

quick_checks.check: Check for unexplained iron-deficiency anemia (fatigue, pale skin, shortness of breath) via basic blood test
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Combined with digestive symptoms, anemia raises suspicion for blood-feeding parasites like hookworms.

quick_checks.check: Observe for itching around the anus, especially at night, and check for tiny worms or debris in underwear or bedding
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Classic presentation of pinworm infection; most common parasitic infection in developed countries.

quick_checks.check: Assess for persistent diarrhea (>2 weeks) with bloating, cramping, and unintentional weight loss
quick_checks.what_it_suggests: Chronic pattern suggests intestinal parasitism; warrants stool testing.

sections.relevance_signal: Symptoms **began after travel**, a stomach bug, or contaminated water. Chronic loose stool, fatigue, and bloating that never fully resolved after an acute gut illness.
